title: Synthesis and characterization of a novel and highly efficient light-emitting polymer
abstract:
Synthesis and characterization of a novel and highly efficient light-emitting
polymer
Y. Liu+#, M.S. Liu+ and A.K.-Y. Jen+*
+
Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University, 360 Huntington Avenue, Boston, MA 02115, USA
#
Present address: Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100080, China
A polymer (TPD-PPV), incorporating both efficient light-emitting and hole transporting moieties has been synthesized. This
polymer also possesses excellent film-forming property, good thermal stability, and high electrochemical reversibility and
stability. The HOMO-LUMO energy levels were determined by cyclic voltammetry and UV-Vis measurement. The diode
with the structure of ITO/CuPc/TPD-PPV/Al showed high rectification ratio (108) and low turn-on voltage (4.2 V). A bright
green-yellow light-emission was observed in day-light under forward bias.
Poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV) reported by Bur-
roughes et al. [1] was the first conjugated polymer that
exhibited a very interesting electroluminescent (EL) phe-
nomenon. The polymer emits light in the green-yellow part
of the spectrum under a low DC driving voltage. Its good
structural properties and reasonably high efficiency make it
a promising light-emitting material candidate for large-area
flat panel displays. However, thin film processing of PPVin
the fabrication of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) is very diffi-
cult due to PPV’s very poor solubility in common organic
solvents. A great deal of effort has been undertaken to
improve its processability by introducing side-chains on the
phenyl ring [2–6]. Among those PPV derivatives, poly(2-
methoxy-5-(29-ethylhexyloxy)-1,4-phenylene
vinylene)
(MEH-PPV) is a well studied material with greatly
improved solubility [7]. Another alternative approach is to
insert a second monomer in the polymer main chain as a
copolymer. Through this copolymerization, not only
improved processability can be achieved, but other desir-
able properties, such as electrochemical reversibility and
stability, carrier transporting, quantum efficiency, lifetime
and color variety can be enhanced. For example, the results
reported by Ho¨rhold that copolymerize a good hole trans-
porting molecule [N,N9-diphenyl-N,N9-bis(3-methylphe-
nyl)-1,19-biphenyl-4,49-diamine] (TPD) into an alkoxyphe-
nylene vinylene polymer backbone significantly improved
the solubility and the charge transporting properties of the
polymers [8]. In thispaper, we report the synthesis and char-
acterization of a novel copolymer based on the modifica-
tions of TPD and phenylene vinylene moieties.
As shown in Scheme 1, compound 1 was synthesized in
55% yield from the modified Buchwald coupling reaction
[9] between aniline and 4,4-dibromobiphenyl in the pres-
ence
of
tris(dibenzylideneacetone)
dipalladium
[Pd2(dba)3], 1,19-bis(diphenylphosphino)ferrocene (dppf)
and sodium tert-butoxide (NaO-t-Bu), followed by the
addition of 1-bromo-4-butylbenzene and additional NaO-
t-Bu. The dialdehyde 2 was obtained in 60% yield using
the Vilsmeier reaction [10]. Compound 3 was obtained in
quantitative yield by the reaction of a,a9-dichloro-p-xylene
with triethylphosphite [11]. The desired copolymer (TPD-
PPV) was prepared by the Wittig-Horner condensation
reaction [12] between the dialdehyde 2 and tetraethyl-1,4-
xylene diphosphonate 3 in tetrahydrofuran (THF) by
slowly adding a solution of 2 eq. of KO-t-Bu in THF as
base. The resulting polymer was subjected to Soxhlet
extraction with acetone to remove any low molecular
weight products.
The molecular weight of the TPD-PPV (M
—
w = 35810)
was measured by gel permeation chromatography (GPC)
with THF as eluent and polystyrene as standard. The poly-
dispersity index was 3.06. The polymer also possesses
both very good solubility in common organic solvents
(chloroform, THF and cyclopentanone) and excellent film
forming property (free-standing film).
Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) of TPD-PPV was
shown in Fig. 1. No weight loss was observed on heating
up to 2908C under nitrogen. The copolymer underwent a
two-step decomposition above 2908C with weight losses
of 5.67% and 16.42%, respectively. The second weight
loss is probably due to the decomposition of butyl groups
(theoretical calculation for the weight loss is 16.16 %).
The glass-transition temperature (Tg) at 1308C was deter-
mined by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) (Fig. 2).
Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is one of the useful methods
for estimating the energy level of organic compounds. The
oxidation process corresponds to the removal of electrons
from the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO),
whereas the reduction cycle corresponds to the filling by
electrons of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital
(LUMO). Therefore, the onset oxidation and reduction
potentials are closely related to the energies of the HOMO
and LUMO levels of a organic molecule and thus can pro-
vide important information regarding the magnitude of the

energy gap. Figure 3 shows the CVs of PPVand TPD-PPV
films on ITO glass. For comparison, the CV measurements
were carried out at the same experimental conditions. A
positive scan revealed two reversible oxidation processes
(EI
1/2 = 0.61 V, EII
1/2 = 0.71 V vs. Ag/Ag+) for TPD-PPV. In
contrast, PPVexhibited two irreversible waves, which dis-
appeared after a few repetitive scans. In the case of TPD-
PPV the anodic peak current, Ipa decreased only slightly
after 10 repetitive scans between 0 to 0.95 V, as shown in
Fig. 4, demonstrating excellent electrochemical stability.
The electrochemical characteristics of TPD, PPV and
TPD-PPV are summarized in Table 1. The energy values
were calculated using the ferrocence (FOC) value of –4.8
eV with respect to the vacuum level which is defined as
zero [13, 14]. It is shown from the table, the HOMO level
of TPD-PPV (–5.16 eV) is 0.42 eV lower than that of PPV
(–5.58 eV) and is very close to that of TPD molecule. This
indicates that the TPD moiety in the polymer may play a
dominating role in determining its oxidation potential.
Moreover, since indium-tin-oxide (ITO) glass (having a
Scheme 1. Synthesis of TPD-PPV.
Fig. 2. Differential scanning calorimetry curve of TPD-PPV.
Fig. 3. Cyclic voltammograms of PPVand TPD-PPV films on ITO
glass in a 0.1 M solution of TBAP in acteonitrile scanned at a rate
of 40 mV/s. Solid line, TPD-PPV film; dotted line, PPV film.

Synthesis and characterization of a novel and highly efficient light-emitting polymer
work function of –4.8 eV) is usually used as anodic elec-
trode, the decrease of energy barrier between ITO glass
and TPD-PPV will greatly facilitate the hole injection,
which in turn will improve the performance of the device.
From UV-Vis measurements, the p-p* band gap for
TPD-PPV was calculated. It is worth to note that the onset
wavelength of TPD-PPV film (Fig. 5), konset = 495 nm cor-
responds to a band gap of 2.51 eV, which is in good agree-
ment with the result obtained by the electrochemical (EC)
The current-voltage (I-V) characteristic of the device
with the configuration of ITO/CuPc/TPD-PPV/Al is illu-
strated in Fig. 6. The device has a low turn-on voltage of
4.2 V, and the current increases superlinearly with increas-
ing of applied voltage. This means that the layer of CuPc
greatly facilitates the injection of holes from the anode of
ITO. A very high rectification ratio of Rr = 3.146108 was
determined from the I-V characteristic at l3.75 V.
The PL spectra of TPD-PPVin both chloroform solution
and as film were measured at the same excitation wave-
length of 366 nm, as shown in Fig. 7. As a result of the for-
mation of aggregates, the emission peak maximum of the
TPD-PPV film was red-shifted by 10 nm as compared to
the solution. A bright green-yellow light-emission was
observed from the device under a forward bias voltage of
7 V. The EL spectrum covers the region between 460 and
640 nm, with a peak at 525 nm and a shoulder at around
In conclusion, we have reported the synthesis and char-
acterization of a high performance copolymer containing
TPD and PPV moieties. In addition to the much improved
solubility as compared to PPV, the copolymer demon-
strates excellent electrochemical reversibility and stability,
low turn-on voltage and high rectification ratio. This may
offer a great potential for this polymer to be employed as a
new and efficient candidate for the light-emitting diodes.
Table 1. Electronic parameters for TPD, PPVand TPD-PPV.
Fig. 4. Cyclic voltammogram of 10 cycles of repetitive scans of
Fig. 5. UV-Vis absorption spectra of TPD-PPV. Solid line, as film;
dotted line, in chloroform solution.
Fig. 6. Current-voltage characteristic of the ITO/CuPc/TPD-PPV/
Fig. 7. Photoluminescence and electroluminescence spectra of
TPD-PPV. Solid line with triangles, PL in chloroform solution;
dotted line, PL as film; solid line, EL of the device.
Electrochemical measurements were performed on a
Voltammetric Analyzer (BAS, CV-50 W) in a three elec-
trode cell with a working electrode of ITO-coated glass
(sheet resistance of 100 X/F), a reference electrode (Ag/
Ag+, non-aqueous) and a counter electrode (Pt gauze)
under nitrogen atmosphere. Films were spin-coated on the
precleaned ITO glass from a chloroform solution (concen-
(TBAP, 0.1 M), and acetonitrile were used as electrolyte
TGA and DSC measurements were performed on a Shi-
madzu Thermal Analyzer (TGA-50 and DSC-50). I-V
characteristics, photo- and electro-luminescence spectra
measurements were carried on a Semiconductor Parameter
Analyzer (Hewlett Packard 4155B) and an InstaSpec IV
spectrometer (Oriel Instruments), respectively.
The EL device was constructed using a double layer
structure with copper phthalocyanine (CuPc) as the hole
transport layer and TPD-PPVas the emitting layer. Prior to
the deposition of these organic films, ITO-coated glass
substrate was cleaned by sequential ultrasonic washing in
detergent solution, deionized water, methanol and chloro-
form for 30 min each, and finally rinsed with acetone for 2
h in a Soxhlet extractor. The CuPc layer was first vacuum
deposited under a pressure of 2.0610–6 Torr onto an ITO-
coated glass substrate, then a thin layer of TPD-PPV (con-
centration of 5 mg/ml in chloroform) was spin-coated onto
the CuPc layer at 2000 rpm for 20 s. Finally, an aluminum
(Al) layer was deposited on the TPD-PPV layer surface as
the top electrode under the same vacuum condition. The
thickness of CuPc, TPD and Al were about 500 A˚ , 500 A˚
and 1000 A˚ , respectively. The emitting area was 0.07 cm2.
PL and EL spectra were measured at room temperature

weight measurement. Financial support from Office of
